speaker: interviewer
text: This is the interview with Dr. Helen Fagin. Today is February 11, 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in charge of the food, the cherries, doling them out, who all was in the attic with you, and how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a number of neighbors who obviously were warned about the action, and they got there much earlier than we did. I would say there were probably between 20 to 30 people there. There was a young child there, which was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very annoying to very many people, unfortunately, because everybody was worried that he would cry and make noises. But we persevered. I really don't remember how many days we were there, but I do remember the anxiety and the trauma that we were going through because we were certain that our parents had not been warned, and we just didn't know what would have been happening to them. Well, when we left the attic, we found out the worst. Our parents have been taken away. Our parents have been taken away with all these people who unexpectedly found themselves totally open to the raids. We could not find out what happened to them, where they were taken. Except the following morning, after we came out, a man from the Judenrat came up to us and presented us with two things. One was my father's silver cigarette case, which he had had. And the sil-- the cigarette case had his and my mother's signatures in gold duplicated, and he had the names of all three of us in gold encrusted there. But these were removed. Just the case was returned. And a little gold medallion from my mother in which there was a little piece of paper, where my mother wrote to us a note saying, dear children, we are so sorry this is happening to us. Whatever you do, make sure you stay together. Take care of yourselves. And this was something that we remembered for the entire duration of our fight for survival. We tried very, very much to stay together. At that time, they diminished the ghetto into a much smaller ghetto. And that was al-- almost the size of a small, small neighborhood street with very, very few houses-- one large house in which we shared a room with a family. And we knew that that was the end. That was coming to an end. All of a sudden, we get a visitor-- a young man who we knew was not Jewish. And he comes to us and he says, I was sent here by the judge, who is a good f-- was a good friend of your father's. And he has heard what happened to your mother and father, and he told me to save the three of you. He was connected with the underground. His name was Henryk, H-E-N-R-Y-K, Wroblewski, W-R-O-B-L-E-W-S-K-I. His name is on the list in the Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC of the rescuers, and he was also honored at the Avenue of the Righteous in Yad Vashem. He came to us and he said, I can't take all three of us together. He looked at the three of us, and he decided that my sister Fela, who was blue-eyed and light-haired, he would take her, and then he would come back for the two of us. Well, we had no choice. He told us not to even discuss it. He took her away, and he says he was taking her to Warsaw. He has connections with the underground and that he will make sure that we are able to be united with her. Well, she left. And soon-- and it was wintertime. And soon we realized that we are left to our own devices. I was able to go on a work detail, and they took me on a work detail one night. It was early January. And all of a sudden, they brought us back in the middle of the night, not allowing us to finish the shift. Oh, I have to backtrack because we realized that they were going to liquidate the ghetto because there were-- there were some comings and goings in the ghetto, and there was a lot of activity. So I decided that I am not going to let my younger sister stick around and, and, and be subjected to this liquidation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told her that I'm going to send her to these friends of ours. Their name was Loszek, L-O-S-Z-E-K. And I said, you will go to the Loszeks, and you will tell them to keep you until Henryk can come for you, for them to get in touch with Henryk. And I remember she had a blue coat, and she had a muff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember when we left very early on, when we went to Konskie, my mother sewed into that muff, of all the ludicrous things, optimistically, her Prudential life insurance-- it was Prudential, the same name as we have here-- my mother's and my father's life insurance, a deed to the property my father had bought in Israel, and a string of real seeded pearls. So I gave my sister the muff, and I told her how important that muff was. And that was dusk. And I took her to a, not a gate, but a, a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't think of the word. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, in a garden, around a garden you have-- a fence. Sorry. There was a fence there, and there was a hole in the fence. And I said to her, you go through that hole, and I'll be waiting. I hope I don't hear any noises. I told her how to get there. And I just said a little prayer to my parents, and I let her go. And there I was left alone. And when I didn't hear any shots, and I didn't hear any guns firing, I was just hoping that she was able to make it. Well, probably a couple of weeks later, the final Aktion came along, when they brought us back from the factory in the middle of the night. And they just took us straight into Umschlagplatz, which is a square where they conducted the selections and the actions. And they told us that we have to form a column of people, five in a row. And it was just dawning, and the light was coming through the sky. And we all looked at each other. We started recognizing each other. And we realized that the columns was-- were never-ending. There were many, many people. And all of a sudden, we realize, here it is. They liquidated the ghetto. They got everybody out of the ghetto. And once they got everybody out of the ghetto, amongst shouts and screams and yells and cries, we started marching. I was in a column with-- holding hands with two of my friends, one of them who had been married. Her husband had been taken away earlier, and she was very weak because she had just suffered a miscarriage. And we were walking through, and we were realizing we were going in the direction of the railroad station. And I was passing a house, which was familiar to me because friends of ours lived there-- had lived there. And I said to my friend to my left, I said, for better or for worse, worse, I think that I'm going to have to escape. I don't know how. And all of a sudden, somebody fainted in the back, and there were shouts. And the-- every 10 rows, an SS man was accompanying us with a machine gun at the ready and with a police dog. And the SS men that were policing the front of the column ran back to the back. And I squeezed my friend's hand, and I ran. And I ran right into the building. And it was a three or four-story building, and I ran up to the top. And I noticed a bin with coal there, and I just crawled into that bin, closed the lid. And I said, whatever happens happens. I could not keep on worrying any longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 20, 21 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had you heard about the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all we heard is that they are taking people away to places from where nobody returns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to believe--

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and the, the conjecture was that these were labor camps, that people are working very hard. We did not know of death camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know of death camps, not at that time, not in January of 1943. The event I am now describing happened January 6, 1943. The reason I remember the date because it was a Catholic, Catholic holiday of All Saints, Wszystkich Swietych. And the, the, the, the town was deserted. And when I was there at the bin, I heard shouting in German, [GERMAN], somebody escaped, and people were running. The SS was running up and down the stairs, and I heard the dogs. And I said to myself, I'm not going to scheme anymore because I knew that wherever I was going I would not return from. And I was just sitting there in that coal bin, squatting, and almost fearful to breathe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really don't know. I remember that I was listening to the noises, and, and I was-- I, I-- really, I was numb. I couldn't scheme any longer. And then when I realized that all the noises abated, and I sort of heard wagons going back and forth on the street, I realized that the streets were free and that people were moving about. And I just crawled out of the bin and shook all the coal off myself. I don't recall what I was wearing at that time. And I don't know. I was very numb. And I was going down the stairs, and I said, I just won't look back. I will just go straight ahead. And it was just about two, two and a half blocks from where the Loszeks lived. And I summoned all the energy and all the nerve, and I went upstairs to the Loszeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Loszek opened the door, and she says-- I'll never forget it-- Jesus Christ, look at her. She's here. And I said, is Tobcia here? And she says, yes, she's sound and safe. But she had her isolated in a room. She put a big chifferobe against a door because it seems that she had some neighbors coming in, and she was afraid that they would snoop her out. So she moved the chifferobe, and she brought me there. And Tobcia was there, and we ran into each other's arms. And that was-- I call it the beginning of the end because we knew we had to fight another battle.

speaker: interviewee
text: We then made plans for the two of us. And I was assured that Henryk will come for Tobcia, and that I would have to wait. Well, Henryk came for Tobcia, but they couldn't keep her-- couldn't keep me there because their life was in danger. So Mrs. Loszek took me to a village, to a barn. And there was a lock on the barn, and she opened the lock, and she brought me there. And she left me some bread and cheese and some water. And she says, Henryk will be back for you in three days. And there was a straw sack there for me to lie down, and there was a big vessel-like that I could eliminate into if I needed. Well, I sort of didn't trust that he will come in three days, so I was very frugal with the food I consumed. He didn't come in three days. He didn't come in seven days. And I finally subsisted on some leftover water and some crumbs. But one recollection I have of that incident, which left me for life with an aversion, my only company were mice. And since then I have a horrible aversion to rodent, to mice and to rats because I didn't trust myself to sleep because I didn't want them to crawl over me. And I hated them. I still hate mice with a passion. I can't stand rodents at all. Well, after the tenth day, I think it was, Mrs. Loszek came in and apologized. She brought me some more food, and she said that Henryk will definitely come for me. And after a few days, he did come for me. He came with a broken-down pickup truck. He says he couldn't trust himself to take me to Warsaw by, by train because they had strict control, and I still didn't have any papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a very, very eventful trip, avoiding all the guards and all the-- all the road blocks and all that. It was really a very good introduction to living on Aryan papers and hiding from place to place. And he brought me to an apartment, and he introduced me to a lady in the apartment, and she was his wife. She was Jewish. So that was a revelation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right then and there he said, we got papers for your sister, and your sister is safe in a safe house, in an underground safe house, a hiding house that the underground maintains. And we have to give you false papers, but they are going to be fake papers because we couldn't get any realistically auth-- authentic papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave papers to my younger sister, and we called her Theresa Wrobelewska, and for me, Sophia, or Zosia, Wrobelewska. And these are our first false papers because we needed false papers should there be a raid on the safe house so we had something to identify ourselves with. And we were taken to that safe house.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we came there, we were there, there was already one couple there, a married couple there. And we came, and the man who was in charge of the apartment-- it was his apartment. It was on a-- in an outskirt of Warsaw, which is called Praga, on the other side of the river Wisla, or Vistula. And he was very much matter of fact. He says, these are the safety rules here. This is-- these are the beds you are going to sleep in. You cannot make any noises. The food is going to be rationed. We don't have much food. And he says, this is a dangerous place to be because I have a clandestine short-wave radio. Well, to make a very long story short, there were a couple of raids while we were there. The SS men came there. They were looking for somebody specifically. And fortunately, we did not answer to that description, and they left. They were in a great hurry. Another emergency was when-- we, we had some kind of a established, established knock on the door that we knew that we could or could not answer. And sure enough, the familiar knock once happened, and we opened the door, and there was a priest. And we realized that we may have blown our cover. But indeed, he was in disguise. And he came to tell us that we can no longer stay there because the neigh-- the neighbors have been very nervous, and there have been some people who are suspecting us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister, older sister, went out. And with the help of the underground, she was able to rent a room under her false name, and she found some kind of an employment. And then the two of us were left alone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I felt that I needed some kind of a way of surviving. I knew I couldn't survive there. So I decided that I better go into the ghetto. What was your physical condit-- condition at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a thin, little girl. I was sustaining myself on as little food as I could. I, I think my thought processes were, at that time, already trained to the point that I was trying to survive psychologically as well as physically. And this is something that I think those of us who had some kind of a intellectual facility and have not been yet totally depressed were able to summon somehow. One person who does it very well, who describes it very well, is Viktor Frankl in his book He, he makes a very, very good statement. He says, no matter how much your physical freedom has been taken away from you, nobody can deprive you of your spiritual freedom. You command your spiritual freedom. You can think your thoughts without anybody realizing what you are thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sooner or later, when you come to a point that you know that depression could set in, you try to save yourself from that depression. How could you do it? You can do it with your inner strength. So you enter into a thought process. And the thought process is either to return to your previous life or to fantasize about your future life. And this is what one has to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: The underground helped me get into the ghetto. I was in the ghetto for some time. And there was a great unrest in the ghetto. Don't forget this is 1943, and the ghetto uprising-- the Jewish ghetto uprising took place April 1943. And we knew something was happening. And we went to a meeting, and somebody made a statement, a general statement. Those of you who think you could survive on the other side, or have reason to go to the other side, do it now because it may be too late. They were able to give me authenticated false paper under the name of Leokadia Jakobiak, who was a girl, a Polish girl, who had been taken for forced labor into Germany, and she was an actual person. And they also gave me some kind of a biographical data as to her parents, whether they are alive or dead, and where she was born, et cetera. I took these papers, and they helped me to get out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got out of the ghetto two weeks before the uprising. And I reunited with Theresa, and we decided that we have to do something with Theresa. I was able to rent a room. And also, with the help of the underground, I was able to get a job. The job that I got was-- the best I could describe, it was the epitome of chutzpah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The job I got was in a German communication company called, called Fernmeltische Staatswerke. You want me to spell it? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: F-E-R-N-M-E-L-T-I-S-C-H-E Fernmeltische, Staatswerke, capital S-T-A-A-T-S-W-E-R-K-E. Staat means country. Werke means works. These were country works. These were governmental works for communication, implementation of communication. Every time I walked into this place, there was a guard with a machine gun. Every time I walked out of the place, I was facing a machine gun by the guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you terrified, or were you just emotionally shut down?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was absolutely terrified, but I just decided one thing. I had to psychologically-- I had to psych myself up. And I said to myself, I am not Jewish. I am not Jewish. I am not Jewish. I am not who I am. I am Leokadia Jakobiak. I am Polish. I am not Jewish. My Jewishness had to be put aside. And I worked there. But before that, I had to do something with my younger sister. And we knew she could not be on the outside because she had very dark hair and very curly black hair, and she was what the Polish would sniff out immediately as a Jewish child. The Poles had a very good nose, and they were very good informants, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister-- my older sister, and I decided that we are going to dye my younger sister's hair to make her blonde. We went to the drugstore. We got peroxide, and we burnt her hair. And it was like straw, and her hair fell out. So we realized we had to shave her hair. And that this an episode which was really critical in my younger sister's life.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know what to do with her. So a fortunate coincidence was that somebody that I knew, a former boyfriend of mine, who was wondering about and came back to Warsaw, was also on Polish papers, on Aryan papers, with his wife, who was also Jewish on Polish papers. And she worked in a potato chip factory, and she told me that the potatoes are delivered to her factory by nuns. The convent has a farm, a potato farm, and they have an orphanage. And if we can concoct some kind of a story, we can say she-- that our younger sister is an orphan.

speaker: interviewer
text: I took her to the Mother Superior. I told her that she suffered meningitis because in Poland when you suffered from meningitis, your, your head is shaved, and that she lost her memory. And that she forgot all her catechism, and that she forgot all of her prayers, and would they take her in? And sure enough, they took her in. And they did enter into refreshing her memory to such an extent that she became a very, very good Catholic, an honest-to-goodness believing Catholic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, in the meantime, roomed with a family, a non-Jewish family, of course, and was working. One of the things that I struck a deal with the Mother Superior for is that since there was an orphanage there of different ages, of girls of different ages, and they don't have education, would she permit me to come once a week on Sunday-- I posed as my sister's aunt-- to teach them something?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that would give me contact with my sister. And indeed, I would come there every Sunday, and I would teach the girls. And I would sort of steal myself away for a few moments with her, and she would tell me a few things about her life and how she felt and all that. In the meantime, while I was with my family, I had to play a very good Catholic as well, and I was going with them to mass every, every Sunday. And I was really integrated into their lifestyle so that I wouldn't act any differently from the way they were acting.
